title: HTML Images: Displaying Images in Web Pages

meta.title: HTML Images Guide – img Tag, Attributes & Optimization (2026)
meta.description: Learn how to display images in HTML using the img tag, attributes like src and alt, responsive images, and best practices.

content.value: What are HTML Images?

HTML images allow you to display pictures, graphics, icons, and illustrations on web pages using the `` tag. Images make websites more engaging, help explain concepts visually, and improve user experience.
💡 The `` tag is an **empty element** — it doesn't have a closing tag. All image information is stored in its attributes.
Basic Syntax
Here's the simplest way to add an image to your webpage:
The browser reads this tag, fetches the image from the specified location, and displays it in your page.
Essential Image Attributes

rows: Image source path (URL or file path)
Alternative text for accessibility & broken images
Image width in pixels or percentage

Understanding Image Paths
The `src` attribute accepts two types of paths:

rows: Images hosted on another website or CDN
Images stored in your own project folder

content.value: Supported Image Formats

rows: Small file size, millions of colors
No transparency, loses quality when compressed
Scalable without quality loss, small size

content.value: Responsive Images (Mobile-Friendly)
Websites must look good on all devices — from smartphones to 4K monitors. Here's how to make images responsive:
Method 1: CSS max-width (Simplest)
This ensures the image never exceeds its container's width while maintaining aspect ratio.
Method 2: srcset (Serves different sizes for different screens)
The browser automatically picks the most appropriate image based on screen size and resolution.
Advanced: The Picture Element
Use `<picture>` when you need different **crops** or entirely different images for different screen sizes (e.g., mobile vs desktop layouts).
📱 The `<picture>` element is perfect for art direction — showing a portrait crop on mobile and a wide landscape on desktop.
Making Images Clickable (Image Links)
Wrap an `` inside an `` tag to turn it into a clickable link:
Image Optimization Best Practices

value: **✅ Always include alt text** – Critical for accessibility (screen readers) and SEO. Describe the image's content or purpose.
**✅ Compress your images** – Use tools like TinyPNG, Squoosh, or ImageOptim to reduce file size without visible quality loss.
**✅ Choose the right format** – JPEG for photos, PNG for logos/transparency, WebP for everything modern.
**✅ Enable lazy loading** – Add `loading="lazy"` to images below the fold to speed up initial page load.
**✅ Specify dimensions** – Always set `width` and `height` to prevent layout shifts (Cumulative Layout Shift optimization).
**✅ Use responsive images** – Serve smaller images on mobile devices to save bandwidth.

content.value: Common Mistakes to Avoid

value: **❌ Missing alt attribute** – Screen reader users won't know what the image shows. Also hurts SEO.
**❌ Using huge unoptimized images** – A 5MB image will make your page slow and frustrate mobile users.
**❌ Incorrect file paths** – Double-check relative paths; `./images/photo.jpg` is different from `images/photo.jpg`.
**❌ Scaling with width/height only** – Downloading a 2000px image and displaying it at 200px wastes bandwidth. Use `srcset` instead.
**❌ Text inside images** – Screen readers can't read text inside JPEGs. Use real HTML text whenever possible.
**❌ Forgetting to set dimensions** – Causes layout shifts (CLS) and poor user experience.

content.value: Accessibility Tips
Making images accessible ensures everyone can understand your content, including people using screen readers:

value: **Descriptive alt text** – `alt="Golden retriever playing in snow"` not `alt="dog"`.
**Empty alt for decorative images** – Use `alt=""` for purely visual images (backgrounds, spacers).
**Longdesc for complex images** – For charts or diagrams, provide a longer description nearby.
**Good contrast** – Ensure text overlaying images has sufficient contrast.

content.value: Real-World Example
Here's a complete, production-ready image implementation:
This example includes modern formats (WebP with fallbacks), responsive sizing, accessibility, and performance optimizations.
Conclusion

Images bring your websites to life, but they must be implemented thoughtfully. By following these best practices — proper `alt` text, responsive techniques, format selection, and optimization — you'll create fast, accessible, and visually appealing web pages that work beautifully on any device.
Remember: A well-optimized image loads instantly and looks great. A poorly implemented image frustrates users and hurts your SEO. Choose wisely!

faq.question: What is the difference between JPEG, PNG, and WebP?
faq.answer: JPEG is best for photos (small file size, no transparency). PNG supports transparency but has larger files. WebP is modern — smaller than both JPEG and PNG while supporting transparency, but older browsers may not support it (though 96%+ do).

faq.question: Why is the alt attribute so important?
faq.answer: Alt text serves three purposes: 1) Accessibility — screen readers describe images to visually impaired users. 2) SEO — search engines understand image content. 3) Fallback — text appears when images fail to load.

faq.question: What is lazy loading and when should I use it?
faq.answer: Lazy loading delays loading images until they're about to appear on screen. Use it for images below the fold (not visible when page first loads). Don't use it for above-the-fold images (hero images, logos) — they should load immediately.

faq.question: How do I make images responsive on mobile devices?
faq.answer: Use `style="max-width: 100%; height: auto;"` for simple scaling, or `srcset` with multiple image sizes. For different crops (art direction), use the `<picture>` element with `media` queries.

faq.question: What happens if I don't specify width and height?
faq.answer: The browser doesn't know how much space to reserve, causing layout shifts as images load (Cumulative Layout Shift). This hurts user experience and Google's Core Web Vitals ranking. Always specify dimensions!

quiz.question: Which HTML tag is used to display images?
quiz.explanation: The tag is the correct HTML element for displaying images. is not valid HTML, and is not a real tag.

quiz.question: Which attribute specifies the image source (path or URL)?
quiz.explanation: The src (source) attribute tells the browser where to find the image file. alt provides alternative text, href is for links.

quiz.question: Which attribute is most important for accessibility and SEO?
quiz.explanation: The alt attribute provides text descriptions for screen readers and search engines. It's essential for accessibility.

quiz.question: What does loading="lazy" do?
options: Delays loading images until they're about to appear on screen
Loads all images at once but with lower quality
quiz.explanation: Lazy loading improves performance by deferring off-screen images. They only load when the user scrolls near them.

quiz.question: Which image format supports transparency and animation?
quiz.explanation: GIF supports both transparency (though limited) and animation. PNG supports transparency but not animation. WebP supports both but is newer.
